pattern: Cyanotic infant with increased pulmonary vascularity

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a widened superior mediastinum with a prominent left-sided vertical vein, left brachiocephalic vein, and dilated superior vena cava, creating a classic 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' appearance. There is associated increased pulmonary vascularity, which may instead present as severe pulmonary edema with a normal heart size in the infradiaphragmatic variant.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include prominent right-sided cardiac chambers due to volume overload and a right-to-left shunt, usually via an obligatory patent foramen ovale or atrial septal defect. The pulmonary vasculature is globally plethoric, reflecting the anomalous venous return.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a narrowed superior mediastinum giving an 'egg on a string' appearance, which would suggest transposition of the great arteries. Furthermore, there is no right-sided aortic arch, which is frequently seen with truncus arteriosus or tetralogy of Fallot.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a 'snowman' cardiomediastinal contour combined with pulmonary plethora in a cyanotic infant.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Total anomalous pulmonary venous return, given the widened superior mediastinum formed by the anomalous left vertical vein and dilated superior vena cava. This creates the classic 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' sign, which alongside pulmonary plethora, is diagnostic of this cyanotic congenital heart disease.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Transposition of the great arteries, however this typically presents with a narrow superior mediastinum and a globular heart, often described as an 'egg on a string' appearance. Additionally, transposition is the most common cyanotic lesion presenting in the first day of life, whereas supracardiac TAPVR often presents slightly later as pulmonary vascular resistance falls.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Truncus arteriosus, given it also presents with cyanosis and increased pulmonary vascular flow, and missing the shared single arterial trunk would severely alter the surgical approach. However, truncus arteriosus frequently presents with a right-sided aortic arch and lacks the specific superior mediastinal widening seen here.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the anomalous pulmonary venous drainage pathway, identify the site of systemic venous connection, and assess for any venous obstruction.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Reviewing fetal echocardiography is useful to determine if the diagnosis was suspected antenatally and to establish the baseline cardiac chamber sizes.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated postnatal echocardiogram as the initial modality, followed by a CT pulmonary angiogram or cardiac MRI, as these modalities are superior here for exquisitely delineating the complex venous anatomy, confirming the presence of an atrial septal defect, and guiding surgical rerouting.
management.ctPhases: I would specify an ECG-gated arterial phase to optimally opacify the pulmonary veins, vertical vein, and right-sided cardiac structures without motion artefact.
management.mriSequences: I would specify velocity-encoded phase-contrast sequences to assess shunt volume and steady-state free precession (SSFP) cine sequences to evaluate right ventricular function and volume overload.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Paediatric Cardiology and Cardiothoracic Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the specific type of TAPVR, the exact drainage site of the confluence, and any evidence of venous obstruction to expedite corrective surgery.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a widened superior mediastinum with a prominent left-sided vertical vein, left brachiocephalic vein, and dilated superior vena cava, creating a classic 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' appearance. There is associated increased pulmonary vascularity, which may instead present as severe pulmonary edema with a normal heart size in the infradiaphragmatic variant. Supporting features include prominent right-sided cardiac chambers due to volume overload and a right-to-left shunt, usually via an obligatory patent foramen ovale or atrial septal defect. The pulmonary vasculature is globally plethoric, reflecting the anomalous venous return. Importantly, there is no evidence of a narrowed superior mediastinum giving an 'egg on a string' appearance, which would suggest transposition of the great arteries. Furthermore, there is no right-sided aortic arch, which is frequently seen with truncus arteriosus or tetralogy of Fallot. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a 'snowman' cardiomediastinal contour combined with pulmonary plethora in a cyanotic infant.

The appearances are most in keeping with Total anomalous pulmonary venous return, given the widened superior mediastinum formed by the anomalous left vertical vein and dilated superior vena cava. This creates the classic 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' sign, which alongside pulmonary plethora, is diagnostic of this cyanotic congenital heart disease. The principle differential would be Transposition of the great arteries, however this typically presents with a narrow superior mediastinum and a globular heart, often described as an 'egg on a string' appearance. Additionally, transposition is the most common cyanotic lesion presenting in the first day of life, whereas supracardiac TAPVR often presents slightly later as pulmonary vascular resistance falls. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Truncus arteriosus, given it also presents with cyanosis and increased pulmonary vascular flow, and missing the shared single arterial trunk would severely alter the surgical approach. However, truncus arteriosus frequently presents with a right-sided aortic arch and lacks the specific superior mediastinal widening seen here.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the anomalous pulmonary venous drainage pathway, identify the site of systemic venous connection, and assess for any venous obstruction. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Reviewing fetal echocardiography is useful to determine if the diagnosis was suspected antenatally and to establish the baseline cardiac chamber sizes. I would recommend a dedicated postnatal echocardiogram as the initial modality, followed by a CT pulmonary angiogram or cardiac MRI, as these modalities are superior here for exquisitely delineating the complex venous anatomy, confirming the presence of an atrial septal defect, and guiding surgical rerouting. I would specify an ECG-gated arterial phase to optimally opacify the pulmonary veins, vertical vein, and right-sided cardiac structures without motion artefact. I would specify velocity-encoded phase-contrast sequences to assess shunt volume and steady-state free precession (SSFP) cine sequences to evaluate right ventricular function and volume overload. This should go to the Paediatric Cardiology and Cardiothoracic Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the specific type of TAPVR, the exact drainage site of the confluence, and any evidence of venous obstruction to expedite corrective surgery.
